/-! 
Self-contained slice for Chapter 5 (reals via Cauchy sequences) sufficient to typecheck
Chapter5.Real.not_zero_pos. We copy only the necessary pieces from Sections 5.1–5.4 and 4.3.
-/

open Lean Elab Tactic

-- Minimal stand-in for the project-specific `grind` tactic used in proof fields.
elab "grind" : tactic => do
  let t ← `(tactic| intro n; intro hn; try simp [hn.le]; try simp [not_le.mpr hn])
  evalTactic t

/-- From Section 4.3: a notion of ε-closeness on ℚ. -/
def Rat.Close (ε : ℚ) (x y : ℚ) : Prop := |x - y| ≤ ε

namespace Chapter5

/-! Section 5.1: Sequences and Cauchy sequences over ℚ -/

/-- Definition 5.1.1 (Sequence). -/
@[ext]
structure Sequence where
  n₀ : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℚ
  vanish : ∀ n < n₀, seq n = 0

/-- Sequences can be viewed as functions `ℤ → ℚ`. -/
instance Sequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun Sequence (fun _ ↦ ℤ → ℚ) where
  coe := fun a ↦ a.seq

/-- A convenient constructor for sequences starting at an arbitrary `n₀`. -/
abbrev Sequence.mk' (n₀ : ℤ) (a : { n // n ≥ n₀ } → ℚ) : Sequence where
  n₀ := n₀
  seq n := if h : n ≥ n₀ then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by
    grind

/-- Start a sequence at a later index `n₁` (junk value if `n₁ < n₀`). -/
abbrev Sequence.from (a : Sequence) (n₁ : ℤ) : Sequence :=
  mk' (max a.n₀ n₁) (fun n ↦ a (n : ℤ))

/-- Convert a function `ℕ → ℚ` into a `Sequence` starting at 0 (as a coercion). -/
instance : Coe (ℕ → ℚ) Sequence where
  coe a :=
    { n₀ := 0
      seq := fun n ↦ if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
      vanish := by
        intro n hn
        have : ¬ n ≥ 0 := by exact not_le.mpr hn
        simp [this] }

/-- Definition 5.1.3 (ε-steadiness) for general starting points. -/
abbrev Rat.Steady (ε : ℚ) (a : Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n ≥ a.n₀, ∀ m ≥ a.n₀, Rat.Close ε (a n) (a m)

/-- Definition 5.1.6 (Eventually ε-steady). -/
abbrev Rat.EventuallySteady (ε : ℚ) (a : Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N ≥ a.n₀, Rat.Steady ε (a.from N)

/-- Definition: Cauchy sequence. -/
abbrev Sequence.IsCauchy (a : Sequence) : Prop := ∀ ε > (0 : ℚ), Rat.EventuallySteady ε a

end Chapter5

/-! Section 5.2: Eventually ε-close sequences and equivalence of sequences -/

abbrev Rat.CloseSeq (ε : ℚ) (a b : Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n, n ≥ a.n₀ → n ≥ b.n₀ → Rat.Close ε (a n) (b n)

abbrev Rat.EventuallyClose (ε : ℚ) (a b : Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N, Rat.CloseSeq ε (a.from N) (b.from N)

namespace Chapter5

/-- Definition 5.2.6 (Equivalent sequences). -/
abbrev Sequence.Equiv (a b : ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∀ ε > (0 : ℚ), Rat.EventuallyClose ε (a : Sequence) (b : Sequence)

end Chapter5

/-! Section 5.3: The construction of the real numbers -/

namespace Chapter5

/-- A class of Cauchy sequences that start at zero. -/
@[ext]
class CauchySequence extends Sequence where
  zero : n₀ = 0
  cauchy : toSequence.IsCauchy

/-- View a `CauchySequence` as a function `ℕ → ℚ`. -/
instance CauchySequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun CauchySequence (fun _ ↦ ℕ → ℚ) where
  coe a n := a.toSequence (n : ℤ)

/-- A sequence starting at zero that is Cauchy, viewed as a Cauchy sequence. -/
abbrev CauchySequence.mk' {a : ℕ → ℚ} (ha : (a : Sequence).IsCauchy) : CauchySequence where
  n₀ := 0
  seq := (a : Sequence).seq
  vanish := by aesop
  zero := rfl
  cauchy := ha

/-- Setoid on Cauchy sequences given by `Sequence.Equiv`. -/
instance CauchySequence.instSetoid : Setoid CauchySequence where
  r := fun a b ↦ Sequence.Equiv a b
  iseqv :=
    { refl := by sorry
      symm := by sorry
      trans := by sorry }

/-- Every constant sequence is Cauchy. -/
theorem Sequence.IsCauchy.const (a : ℚ) : ((fun _ : ℕ ↦ a) : Sequence).IsCauchy := by
  sorry

/-- Zero Cauchy sequence. -/
instance CauchySequence.instZero : Zero CauchySequence where
  zero := CauchySequence.mk' (a := fun _ : ℕ ↦ 0) (Sequence.IsCauchy.const (0 : ℚ))

/-- The real numbers: reals are Cauchy sequences modulo equivalence. -/
abbrev Real := Quotient CauchySequence.instSetoid

open Classical in
/-- Formal limit of a sequence (dummy value 0 if not Cauchy). -/
noncomputable abbrev LIM (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Real :=
  Quotient.mk _ (if h : (a : Sequence).IsCauchy then CauchySequence.mk' h else (0 : CauchySequence))

/-- Rationals embed into reals via constant Cauchy sequences. -/
instance Real.instRatCast : RatCast Real where
  ratCast := fun q ↦ Quotient.mk _ (CauchySequence.mk' (a := fun _ ↦ q) (Sequence.IsCauchy.const q))

/-- Natural numbers embed into reals via `ℕ ⊆ ℚ ⊆ Real`. -/
instance Real.instOfNat {n : ℕ} : OfNat Real n where ofNat := ((n : ℚ) : Real)

end Chapter5

/-! Section 5.4: Sign on the reals -/

namespace Chapter5

/-- Definition 5.4.1 (sequences bounded away from zero with sign, positive). -/
abbrev BoundedAwayPos (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Prop := ∃ c : ℚ, c > 0 ∧ ∀ n, a n ≥ c

/-- Definition 5.4.1 (positive reals via Cauchy sequences bounded away from 0 on the right). -/
abbrev Real.IsPos (x : Real) : Prop :=
  ∃ a : ℕ → ℚ, BoundedAwayPos a ∧ (a : Sequence).IsCauchy ∧ x = LIM a

/-- Unfolding lemma for `IsPos`. -/
lemma Real.isPos_def (x : Real) : IsPos x ↔ ∃ a : ℕ → ℚ, BoundedAwayPos a ∧ (a : Sequence).IsCauchy ∧ x = LIM a := by
  rfl

/-- Proposition 5.4.4 (basic properties of positive reals) / Exercise 5.4.1 -/
 theorem Real.not_zero_pos (x:Real) : ¬(x = 0 ∧ x.IsPos) := by sorry

end Chapter5

import Mathlib.Tactic

/-!
Self-contained slice for Chapter 5.3 target theorem Real.LIM.harmonic.
We include only the minimal dependencies from Sections 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 4.3
needed for parsing and typechecking the target statement.
-/

/-- From Section 4.3: definition of ε-closeness on ℚ. -/
def Rat.Close (ε : ℚ) (x y : ℚ) := |x - y| ≤ ε

namespace Chapter5

/-- Section 5.1: definition of sequences of rationals. -/
@[ext]
structure Sequence where
  n₀ : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℚ
  vanish : ∀ n < n₀, seq n = 0

/-- Sequences can be thought of as functions from ℤ to ℚ. -/
instance Sequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun Sequence (fun _ ↦ ℤ → ℚ) where
  coe := fun a ↦ a.seq

/--
Functions from ℕ to ℚ can be thought of as sequences starting from 0; `ofNatFun` performs this conversion.

The `coe` attribute allows the delaborator to print `Sequence.ofNatFun f` as `↑f`, which is more concise; you may safely remove this if you prefer the more explicit notation.
-/
@[coe]
def Sequence.ofNatFun (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Sequence where
    n₀ := 0
    seq n := if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
    vanish := by
      grind

/-- If `a : ℕ → ℚ` is used in a context where a `Sequence` is expected, automatically coerce. -/
instance : Coe (ℕ → ℚ) Sequence where
  coe := Sequence.ofNatFun

/-- A convenient constructor for sequences starting at an arbitrary index. -/
abbrev Sequence.mk' (n₀:ℤ) (a: { n // n ≥ n₀ } → ℚ) : Sequence where
  n₀ := n₀
  seq n := if h : n ≥ n₀ then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by grind

/-- Starting a sequence from a given index. -/
abbrev Sequence.from (a:Sequence) (n₁:ℤ) : Sequence :=
  mk' (max a.n₀ n₁) (fun n ↦ a (n:ℤ))

@[simp]
lemma Sequence.n0_coe (a: ℕ → ℚ) : (a:Sequence).n₀ = 0 := rfl

@[aesop safe apply]
lemma Sequence.vanish_coe (a:ℕ→ℚ) : ∀ n, n < (0:ℤ) → (a:Sequence).seq n = 0 := by
  intro n h; simp [Sequence.ofNatFun, h]

end Chapter5

/-- From Section 5.2: auxiliary notions for eventual closeness of sequences. -/
abbrev Rat.CloseSeq (ε: ℚ) (a b: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n, n ≥ a.n₀ → n ≥ b.n₀ → ε.Close (a n) (b n)

abbrev Rat.EventuallyClose (ε: ℚ) (a b: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N, ε.CloseSeq (a.from N) (b.from N)

/-- A slight generalization of Definition 5.1.3: ε-steadiness for sequences. -/
abbrev Rat.Steady (ε: ℚ) (a: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n ≥ a.n₀, ∀ m ≥ a.n₀, ε.Close (a n) (a m)

/-- Definition 5.1.6 (Eventually ε-steady). -/
abbrev Rat.EventuallySteady (ε: ℚ) (a: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N ≥ a.n₀, ε.Steady (a.from N)

namespace Chapter5

/-- Section 5.1: definition of Cauchy sequences (for a sequence starting at arbitrary n₀). -/
abbrev Sequence.IsCauchy (a:Sequence) : Prop := ∀ ε > (0:ℚ), ε.EventuallySteady a

/-- Section 5.2: definition of equivalent sequences (eventual closeness). -/
abbrev Sequence.Equiv (a b: ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∀ ε > (0:ℚ), ε.EventuallyClose (a:Sequence) (b:Sequence)

/-- A class of Cauchy sequences that start at zero. -/
@[ext]
class CauchySequence extends Sequence where
  zero : n₀ = 0
  cauchy : toSequence.IsCauchy

/-- A sequence starting at zero that is Cauchy can be viewed as a Cauchy sequence. -/
abbrev CauchySequence.mk' {a:ℕ → ℚ} (ha: (a:Sequence).IsCauchy) : CauchySequence where
  n₀ := 0
  seq := (a:Sequence).seq
  vanish := by aesop
  zero := rfl
  cauchy := ha

/-- Coerce a Cauchy sequence to its underlying function ℕ → ℚ. -/
instance CauchySequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun CauchySequence (fun _ ↦ ℕ → ℚ) where
  coe a n := a.toSequence (n:ℤ)

/-- Proposition: every constant sequence is Cauchy. -/
lemma Sequence.IsCauchy.const (a:ℚ) : ((fun _ : ℕ ↦ a):Sequence).IsCauchy := by
  -- exact proof omitted in this slice
  sorry

/-- The equivalence relation identifying equivalent Cauchy sequences. -/
instance CauchySequence.instSetoid : Setoid CauchySequence where
  r := fun a b ↦ Sequence.Equiv a b
  iseqv :=
    { refl := by
        -- proof omitted in this slice
        sorry
      symm := by
        -- proof omitted in this slice
        sorry
      trans := by
        -- proof omitted in this slice
        sorry }

/-- Zero Cauchy sequence (the constant zero sequence). -/
instance CauchySequence.instZero : Zero CauchySequence where
  zero := CauchySequence.mk' (a := fun _ : ℕ ↦ 0) (Sequence.IsCauchy.const (0:ℚ))

/-- The real numbers are (quotients of) Cauchy sequences modulo equivalence. -/
abbrev Real := Quotient CauchySequence.instSetoid

open Classical in
/--
  It is convenient in Lean to assign the "dummy" value of 0 to `LIM a` when `a` is not Cauchy.
  This requires Classical logic, because the property of being Cauchy is not computable or
  decidable.
-/
noncomputable abbrev LIM (a:ℕ → ℚ) : Real :=
  Quotient.mk _ (if h : (a:Sequence).IsCauchy then CauchySequence.mk' h else (0:CauchySequence))

/-- Embed the rationals into the reals via constant Cauchy sequences. -/
instance Real.instRatCast : RatCast Real where
  ratCast := fun q ↦
    Quotient.mk _ (CauchySequence.mk' (a := fun _ ↦ q) (Sequence.IsCauchy.const q))

/-- Provide numerals (in particular 0) for Real via casting from ℚ. -/
instance Real.instOfNat {n:ℕ} : OfNat Real n where
  ofNat := ((n:ℚ):Real)

/-- Exercise 5.3.5 -/
 theorem Real.LIM.harmonic : LIM (fun n ↦ 1/((n:ℚ)+1)) = 0 := by
  sorry

end Chapter5

import Mathlib.Tactic

namespace Chapter3

universe u v

/-- The axioms of Zermelo-Frankel theory with atoms.  -/
class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u -- Axiom 3.1
  Object : Type v -- Axiom 3.1
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.1
  mem : Object → Set → Prop -- Axiom 3.1
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y -- Axiom 3.2
  emptyset: Set -- Axiom 3.3
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset -- Axiom 3.3
  singleton : Object → Set -- Axiom 3.4
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y -- Axiom 3.4
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.5
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y) -- Axiom 3.5
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set -- Axiom 3.6
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x -- Axiom 3.6
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set -- Axiom 3.7
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y -- Axiom 3.7
  nat : Set -- Axiom 3.8
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat) -- Axiom 3.8
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S -- Axiom 3.9
  pow : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.11
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.11
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F -- Axiom 3.11
  union : Set → Set -- Axiom 3.12
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A -- Axiom 3.12

-- This enables one to use `Set` and `Object` instead of `SetTheory.Set` and `SetTheory.Object`.
export SetTheory (Set Object nat)

-- This instance implicitly imposes the axioms of Zermelo-Frankel set theory with atoms.
variable [SetTheory]

/-- Definition 3.1.1 (objects can be elements of sets) -/
instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := mem x X

/-- Axiom 3.1 (Sets are objects)-/
instance SetTheory.sets_are_objects : Coe Set Object where
  coe X := set_to_object X

instance SetTheory.Set.instSingleton : Singleton Object Set where
  singleton := singleton

instance SetTheory.Set.instUnion : Union Set where
  union := union_pair

instance SetTheory.Set.instInsert : Insert Object Set where
  insert x X := {x} ∪ X

/-- Definition 3.5.1 (Ordered pair).  One could also have used `Object × Object` to
define `OrderedPair` here. -/
@[ext]
structure OrderedPair where
  fst: Object
  snd: Object

/-- Exercise 3.5.1, first part -/
def OrderedPair.toObject : OrderedPair ↪ Object where
  toFun p := ({ (({p.fst}:Set):Object), (({p.fst, p.snd}:Set):Object) }:Set)
  inj' := by sorry

end Chapter3

namespace Chapter11

inductive BoundedInterval where
  | Ioo (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Icc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ioc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ico (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval

open BoundedInterval

/-- There is a technical issue in that this coercion is not injective: the empty set is represented by multiple bounded intervals.  This causes some of the statements in this section to be a little uglier than necessary.-/
@[coe]
def BoundedInterval.toSet (I: BoundedInterval) : Set ℝ := match I with
  | Ioo a b => .Ioo a b
  | Icc a b => .Icc a b
  | Ioc a b => .Ioc a b
  | Ico a b => .Ico a b

instance BoundedInterval.inst_coeSet : Coe BoundedInterval (Set ℝ) where
  coe := toSet

instance BoundedInterval.instMembership : Membership ℝ BoundedInterval where
  mem I x := x ∈ (I:Set ℝ)

instance BoundedInterval.instSubset : HasSubset BoundedInterval where
  Subset I J := ∀ x, x ∈ I → x ∈ J

@[ext]
structure Partition (I: BoundedInterval) where
  intervals : Finset BoundedInterval
  exists_unique (x:ℝ) (hx : x ∈ I) : ∃! J, J ∈ intervals ∧ x ∈ J
  contains (J : BoundedInterval) (hJ : J ∈ intervals) : J ⊆ I

instance Partition.instMembership (I: BoundedInterval) : Membership BoundedInterval (Partition I) where
  mem P J := J ∈ P.intervals

end Chapter11

namespace Chapter11
open BoundedInterval

/-- Definition 11.2.1 -/
abbrev Constant {X Y:Type} (f: X → Y) : Prop := ∃ c, ∀ x, f x = c

abbrev ConstantOn (f: ℝ → ℝ) (X: Set ℝ) : Prop := Constant (fun x : X ↦ f ↑x)

/-- Definition 11.2.3 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantWith (f:ℝ → ℝ) {I: BoundedInterval} (P: Partition I) : Prop := ∀ J ∈ P, ConstantOn f (J:Set ℝ)

/-- Definition 11.2.5 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantOn (f:ℝ → ℝ) (I: BoundedInterval) : Prop := ∃ P : Partition I, PiecewiseConstantWith f P

/-- Lemma 11.2.8 / Exercise 11.2.2 -/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.sub {f g: ℝ → ℝ} {I: BoundedInterval}
  (hf: PiecewiseConstantOn f I) (hg: PiecewiseConstantOn g I) : PiecewiseConstantOn (f - g) I := by
  sorry

end Chapter11

/-!
Self-contained slice for Chapter 5 target: Real.IsBounded.equiv
-/

namespace Chapter5

/--
  Definition 5.1.1 (Sequence). To avoid some technicalities involving dependent types, we extend
  sequences by zero to the left of the starting point `n₀`.
-/
@[ext]
structure Sequence where
  n₀ : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℚ
  vanish : ∀ n < n₀, seq n = 0

/-- Sequences can be thought of as functions from ℤ to ℚ. -/
instance Sequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun Sequence (fun _ ↦ ℤ → ℚ) where
  coe := fun a ↦ a.seq

/--
Functions from ℕ to ℚ can be thought of as sequences starting from 0; `ofNatFun` performs this conversion.

The `coe` attribute allows the delaborator to print `Sequence.ofNatFun f` as `↑f`, which is more concise; you may safely remove this if you prefer the more explicit notation.
-/
@[coe]
def Sequence.ofNatFun (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Sequence where
    n₀ := 0
    seq n := if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
    vanish := by grind

/--
If `a : ℕ → ℚ` is used in a context where a `Sequence` is expected, automatically coerce `a` to `Sequence.ofNatFun a` (which will be pretty-printed as `↑a`)
-/
instance : Coe (ℕ → ℚ) Sequence where
  coe := Sequence.ofNatFun

abbrev Sequence.mk' (n₀:ℤ) (a: { n // n ≥ n₀ } → ℚ) : Sequence where
  n₀ := n₀
  seq n := if h : n ≥ n₀ then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by grind

lemma Sequence.eval_mk {n n₀:ℤ} (a: { n // n ≥ n₀ } → ℚ) (h: n ≥ n₀) :
    (Sequence.mk' n₀ a) n = a ⟨ n, h ⟩ := by grind

@[simp]
lemma Sequence.n0_coe (a: ℕ → ℚ) : (a:Sequence).n₀ = 0 := by norm_cast

/--
  a.from n₁ starts `a:Sequence` from `n₁`.  It is intended for use when `n₁ ≥ n₀`, but returns
  the "junk" value of the original sequence `a` otherwise.
-/
abbrev Sequence.from (a:Sequence) (n₁:ℤ) : Sequence :=
  mk' (max a.n₀ n₁) (fun n ↦ a (n:ℤ))

/-- Definition 5.1.12 (bounded sequences) -/
abbrev Sequence.BoundedBy (a:Sequence) (M:ℚ) : Prop := ∀ n, |a n| ≤ M

/-- Definition 5.1.12 (bounded sequences) -/
lemma Sequence.boundedBy_def (a:Sequence) (M:ℚ) : a.BoundedBy M ↔ ∀ n, |a n| ≤ M := by rfl

abbrev Sequence.IsBounded (a:Sequence) : Prop := ∃ M ≥ 0, a.BoundedBy M

/-- Definition 5.1.12 (bounded sequences) -/
lemma Sequence.isBounded_def (a:Sequence) : a.IsBounded ↔ ∃ M ≥ 0, a.BoundedBy M := by rfl

end Chapter5

/--
  This definition needs to be made outside of the Section 4.3 namespace for technical reasons.
-/
def Rat.Close (ε : ℚ) (x y:ℚ) := |x-y| ≤ ε

abbrev Rat.CloseSeq (ε: ℚ) (a b: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n, n ≥ a.n₀ → n ≥ b.n₀ → ε.Close (a n) (b n)

abbrev Rat.EventuallyClose (ε: ℚ) (a b: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N, ε.CloseSeq (a.from N) (b.from N)

namespace Chapter5

/-- Definition 5.2.6 (Equivalent sequences) -/
abbrev Sequence.Equiv (a b: ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∀ ε > (0:ℚ), ε.EventuallyClose (a:Sequence) (b:Sequence)

/-- Exercise 5.3.4 -/
theorem Real.IsBounded.equiv {a b:ℕ → ℚ} (ha: (a:Sequence).IsBounded) (hab: Sequence.Equiv a b) :
    (b:Sequence).IsBounded := by sorry

end Chapter5

/-!
Self-contained slice: minimal structures and abbreviations for Chapter7.Series
sufficient to state the target theorem from Chapter8.Section 8.2.
-/

namespace Chapter7

open BigOperators

/--
  Definition 7.2.1 (Formal infinite series). This is similar to Chapter 6 sequence, but is
  manipulated differently. As with Chapter 5, we will start series from 0 by default.
-/
@[ext]
structure Series where
  m : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℝ
  vanish : ∀ n < m, seq n = 0

/-- Functions from ℕ to ℝ can be thought of as series. -/
instance Series.instCoe : Coe (ℕ → ℝ) Series where
  coe := fun a ↦ {
    m := 0
    seq n := if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
    vanish := by grind
  }

@[simp]
theorem Series.eval_coe (a: ℕ → ℝ) (n: ℕ) : (a: Series).seq n = a n := by simp

abbrev Series.mk' {m:ℤ} (a: { n // n ≥ m } → ℝ) : Series where
  m := m
  seq n := if h : n ≥ m then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by grind

theorem Series.eval_mk' {m:ℤ} (a : { n // n ≥ m } → ℝ) {n : ℤ} (h:n ≥ m) :
    (Series.mk' a).seq n = a ⟨ n, h ⟩ := by simp [h]

/-- Definition 7.2.2 (Convergence of series) -/
abbrev Series.partial (s : Series) (N:ℤ) : ℝ := ∑ n ∈ Finset.Icc s.m N, s.seq n

abbrev Series.convergesTo (s : Series) (L:ℝ) : Prop := Filter.atTop.Tendsto (s.partial) (nhds L)

abbrev Series.converges (s : Series) : Prop := ∃ L, s.convergesTo L

/-- Definition 7.2.8 (Absolute convergence) -/
abbrev Series.abs (s:Series) : Series := mk' (m:=s.m) (fun n ↦ |s.seq n|)

abbrev Series.absConverges (s:Series) : Prop := s.abs.converges

end Chapter7

namespace Chapter8

open Chapter7 Chapter7.Series Finset Function Filter

/-- Theorem 8.2.8 (Riemann rearrangement theorem) / Exercise 8.2.5 -/
theorem permute_convergesTo_of_divergent {a: ℕ → ℝ} (ha: (a:Series).converges)
  (ha': ¬ (a:Series).absConverges) (L:ℝ) :
  ∃ f : ℕ → ℕ, Bijective f ∧ (a ∘ f:Series).convergesTo L
  := by
  sorry

end Chapter8

namespace Chapter3

universe u v

/-- The axioms of Zermelo-Frankel theory with atoms.  -/
class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u -- Axiom 3.1
  Object : Type v -- Axiom 3.1
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.1
  mem : Object → Set → Prop -- Axiom 3.1
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y -- Axiom 3.2
  emptyset: Set -- Axiom 3.3
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset -- Axiom 3.3
  singleton : Object → Set -- Axiom 3.4
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y -- Axiom 3.4
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.5
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y) -- Axiom 3.5
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set -- Axiom 3.6
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x -- Axiom 3.6
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set -- Axiom 3.7
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y -- Axiom 3.7
  nat : Set -- Axiom 3.8
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat) -- Axiom 3.8
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S -- Axiom 3.9
  pow : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.11
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.11
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F -- Axiom 3.11
  union : Set → Set -- Axiom 3.12
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A -- Axiom 3.12

export SetTheory (Set Object nat)

variable [SetTheory]

/-- Definition 3.1.1 (objects can be elements of sets) -/
instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := SetTheory.mem x X

/-- Sets are objects. -/
instance SetTheory.sets_are_objects : Coe Set Object where
  coe X := SetTheory.set_to_object X

/-- This defines the subtype `A.toSubtype` for any `A:Set`. -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.toSubtype (A:Set) := Subtype (fun x ↦ x ∈ A)

instance : CoeSort (Set) (Type v) where
  coe A := SetTheory.Set.toSubtype A

abbrev SetTheory.Set.specify (A:Set) (P: A → Prop) : Set := SetTheory.specify A P

instance SetTheory.Set.instIntersection : Inter Set where
  inter X Y := X.specify (fun x ↦ x.val ∈ Y)

instance SetTheory.Set.instSingleton : Singleton Object Set where
  singleton := SetTheory.singleton

instance SetTheory.Set.instUnion : Union Set where
  union := SetTheory.union_pair

instance SetTheory.Set.instInsert : Insert Object Set where
  insert x X := {x} ∪ X

abbrev SetTheory.Set.replace (A:Set) {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP : ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set := SetTheory.replace A P hP

/-- Ordered pairs -/ 
@[ext]
structure OrderedPair where
  fst : Object
  snd : Object

/-- Ordered pair as an object (using a Kuratowski-style encoding). -/
def OrderedPair.toObject : OrderedPair ↪ Object where
  toFun p := ({ (({p.fst}:Set):Object), (({p.fst, p.snd}:Set):Object) }:Set)
  inj' := by
    sorry

instance OrderedPair.inst_coeObject : Coe OrderedPair Object where
  coe := OrderedPair.toObject

/-- A technical operation `{x} × Y` used to define the full Cartesian product -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.slice (x:Object) (Y:Set) : Set :=
  Y.replace (P := fun y z ↦ z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair)) (by
    intro x y y' h
    rcases h with ⟨h1, h2⟩
    simpa [h1, h2]
  )

/-- Cartesian product -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.cartesian (X Y:Set) : Set :=
  SetTheory.union (X.replace (P := fun x z ↦ z = slice x Y) (by
    intro x z z' h
    rcases h with ⟨h1, h2⟩
    exact Eq.trans h1 h2.symm
  ))

/-- This instance enables the ×ˢ notation for Cartesian product. -/
instance SetTheory.Set.inst_SProd : SProd Set Set Set where
  sprod := SetTheory.Set.cartesian

/-- Exercise 3.5.5 -/ 
 theorem SetTheory.Set.inter_of_prod (A B C D:Set) :
    (A ×ˢ B) ∩ (C ×ˢ D) = (A ∩ C) ×ˢ (B ∩ D) := by
  sorry

end Chapter3

namespace Chapter3

universe u v

/-- The axioms of Zermelo-Frankel theory with atoms.  -/
class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u -- Axiom 3.1
  Object : Type v -- Axiom 3.1
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.1
  mem : Object → Set → Prop -- Axiom 3.1
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y -- Axiom 3.2
  emptyset: Set -- Axiom 3.3
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset -- Axiom 3.3
  singleton : Object → Set -- Axiom 3.4
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y -- Axiom 3.4
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.5
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y) -- Axiom 3.5
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set -- Axiom 3.6
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x -- Axiom 3.6
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set -- Axiom 3.7
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y -- Axiom 3.7
  nat : Set -- Axiom 3.8
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat) -- Axiom 3.8
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S -- Axiom 3.9
  pow : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.11
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.11
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F -- Axiom 3.11
  union : Set → Set -- Axiom 3.12
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A -- Axiom 3.12

export SetTheory (Set Object)

variable [SetTheory]

/-- Definition 3.1.1 (objects can be elements of sets) -/
instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := SetTheory.mem x X

/-- Axiom 3.1 (Sets are objects)-/ 
instance SetTheory.sets_are_objects : Coe Set Object where
  coe X := SetTheory.set_to_object X

instance SetTheory.Set.instEmpty : EmptyCollection Set where
  emptyCollection := SetTheory.emptyset

/-- Axiom 3.3 (empty set). -/
@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.not_mem_empty : ∀ x, x ∉ (∅:Set) := SetTheory.emptyset_mem

/-- Axiom 3.2 (Equality of sets).  The `[ext]` tag allows the `ext` tactic to work for sets. -/
@[ext]
theorem SetTheory.Set.ext {X Y:Set} (h: ∀ x, x ∈ X ↔ x ∈ Y) : X = Y :=
  SetTheory.extensionality _ _ h

/-- This defines the subtype `A.toSubtype` for any `A:Set`. -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.toSubtype (A:Set) := Subtype (fun x ↦ x ∈ A)

instance : CoeSort (Set) (Type v) where
  coe A := SetTheory.Set.toSubtype A

abbrev SetTheory.Set.specify (A:Set) (P: A → Prop) : Set := SetTheory.specify A P

instance SetTheory.Set.instSDiff : SDiff Set where
  sdiff X Y := X.specify (fun x ↦ x.val ∉ Y)

abbrev SetTheory.Set.replace (A:Set) {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP : ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set := SetTheory.replace A P hP

/-- Lemma 3.1.5 (Single choice) -/
lemma SetTheory.Set.nonempty_def {X:Set} (h: X ≠ ∅) : ∃ x, x ∈ X := by
  -- This proof is written to follow the structure of the original text.
  by_contra! this
  have claim (x:Object) : x ∈ X ↔ x ∈ (∅:Set) := by simp [this, not_mem_empty]
  apply ext at claim
  contradiction

/-- Indexed intersection (auxiliary choice of index) -/
noncomputable abbrev SetTheory.Set.nonempty_choose {I:Set} (hI: I ≠ ∅) : I :=
  ⟨(nonempty_def hI).choose, (nonempty_def hI).choose_spec⟩

abbrev SetTheory.Set.iInter' (I:Set) (β:I) (A: I → Set) : Set :=
  (A β).specify (P := fun x ↦ ∀ α:I, x.val ∈ A α)

noncomputable abbrev SetTheory.Set.iInter (I: Set) (hI: I ≠ ∅) (A: I → Set) : Set :=
  SetTheory.Set.iInter' I (SetTheory.Set.nonempty_choose hI) A

/-- Indexed union -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.iUnion (I: Set) (A: I → Set) : Set :=
  SetTheory.union (I.replace (P := fun α S ↦ S = A α)
    (by intro _ _ _ ⟨h1, h2⟩; exact h1.trans h2.symm))

/-- Exercise 3.4.11 -/
 theorem SetTheory.Set.compl_iUnion {X I: Set} (hI: I ≠ ∅) (A: I → Set) :
    X \ iUnion I A = iInter I hI (fun α ↦ X \ A α) := by sorry

end Chapter3

Self-contained slice for Chapter 8.2 target theorem.
We reproduce only the minimal Series API used in the statement and the
AbsConvergent predicate from Chapter 8.

/- A lightweight stand-in for the `grind` tactic used in textbook proofs.
   It only needs to solve very simple goals (typically `simp`-able) appearing
   inside structure fields below. -/
macro "grind" : tactic => `(tactic| (first | intros; try simp [*] at *; try simp [*] | aesop?))

namespace Chapter7

open BigOperators

/-- Definition 7.2.1 (Formal infinite series). -/
@[ext]
structure Series where
  m : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℝ
  vanish : ∀ n < m, seq n = 0

/-- Functions from ℕ to ℝ can be thought of as series. -/
instance Series.instCoe : Coe (ℕ → ℝ) Series where
  coe := fun a ↦ {
    m := 0
    seq n := if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
    vanish := by grind
  }

@[simp]
theorem Series.eval_coe (a: ℕ → ℝ) (n: ℕ) : (a: Series).seq n = a n := by simp

abbrev Series.mk' {m:ℤ} (a: { n // n ≥ m } → ℝ) : Series where
  m := m
  seq n := if h : n ≥ m then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by grind

/-- Definition 7.2.2 (Convergence of series) -/
abbrev Series.partial (s : Series) (N:ℤ) : ℝ := ∑ n ∈ Finset.Icc s.m N, s.seq n

abbrev Series.convergesTo (s : Series) (L:ℝ) : Prop := Filter.atTop.Tendsto (s.partial) (nhds L)

abbrev Series.converges (s : Series) : Prop := ∃ L, s.convergesTo L

/-- Definition 7.2.8 (Absolute convergence) -/
abbrev Series.abs (s:Series) : Series := mk' (m:=s.m) (fun n ↦ |s.seq n|)

abbrev Series.absConverges (s:Series) : Prop := s.abs.converges

end Chapter7

namespace Chapter8
open Chapter7 Chapter7.Series Function

/-- Definition 8.2.1 (Series on countable sets). -/
abbrev AbsConvergent {X:Type} (f: X → ℝ) : Prop := ∃ g: ℕ → X, Bijective g ∧ (f ∘ g: Series).absConverges

/-- Lemma 8.2.7 / Exercise 8.2.4 -/
theorem divergent_parts_of_divergent {a: ℕ → ℝ} (ha: (a:Series).converges)
  (ha': ¬ (a:Series).absConverges) :
  ¬ AbsConvergent (fun n : {n | a n ≥ 0} ↦ a n) ∧ ¬ AbsConvergent (fun n : {n | a n < 0} ↦ a n)
  := by
  sorry

end Chapter8

import Mathlib.Tactic

namespace Chapter3

/-- Provide a lightweight alias for the custom tactic `grind` used in the textbook. -/
macro "grind" : tactic => `(tactic| aesop)

universe u v

class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u -- Axiom 3.1
  Object : Type v -- Axiom 3.1
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.1
  mem : Object → Set → Prop -- Axiom 3.1
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y -- Axiom 3.2
  emptyset: Set -- Axiom 3.3
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset -- Axiom 3.3
  singleton : Object → Set -- Axiom 3.4
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y -- Axiom 3.4
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.5
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y) -- Axiom 3.5
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set -- Axiom 3.6
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x -- Axiom 3.6
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set -- Axiom 3.7
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y -- Axiom 3.7
  nat : Set -- Axiom 3.8
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat) -- Axiom 3.8
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S -- Axiom 3.9
  pow : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.11
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.11
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F -- Axiom 3.11
  union : Set → Set -- Axiom 3.12
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A -- Axiom 3.12

export SetTheory (Set Object)

variable [SetTheory]

instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := SetTheory.mem x X

instance SetTheory.sets_are_objects : Coe Set Object where
  coe X := SetTheory.set_to_object X

namespace SetTheory
namespace Set

/-- Unions and singletons, to enable `{x}` and `X ∪ Y` notations (Section 3.1). -/
instance instUnion : Union Set where union := SetTheory.union_pair

instance instSingleton : Singleton Object Set where singleton := SetTheory.singleton

instance instInsert : Insert Object Set where insert x X := {x} ∪ X

end Set
end SetTheory

@[ext]
structure OrderedPair where
  fst: Object
  snd: Object

@[simp]
theorem OrderedPair.eq (x y x' y' : Object) :
    (⟨ x, y ⟩ : OrderedPair) = (⟨ x', y' ⟩ : OrderedPair) ↔ x = x' ∧ y = y' := by aesop

/-- Exercise 3.5.1, first part -/ 
-- (Appears with a `sorry` in the original source.)
def OrderedPair.toObject : OrderedPair ↪ Object where
  toFun p := ({ (({p.fst}:Set):Object), (({p.fst, p.snd}:Set):Object) }:Set)
  inj' := by sorry

instance OrderedPair.inst_coeObject : Coe OrderedPair Object where
  coe := OrderedPair.toObject

namespace SetTheory
namespace Set

abbrev toSubtype (A:Set) := Subtype (fun x ↦ x ∈ A)

instance : CoeSort (Set) (Type v) where
  coe A := toSubtype A

abbrev specify (A:Set) (P: A → Prop) : Set := SetTheory.specify A P

abbrev replace (A:Set) {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP : ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set := SetTheory.replace A P hP

instance instIntersection : Inter Set where
  inter X Y := specify X (fun x ↦ x.val ∈ Y)

/-- A technical operation used to define Cartesian product (Section 3.5). -/
abbrev slice (x:Object) (Y:Set) : Set :=
  Y.replace (P := fun y z ↦ z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair)) (by grind)

/-- Cartesian product (Definition 3.5.4). -/
abbrev cartesian (X Y:Set) : Set :=
  SetTheory.union (X.replace (P := fun x z ↦ z = slice x Y)
    (by intro _ _ _ ⟨h1, h2⟩; exact h1.trans h2.symm))

end Set
end SetTheory

-- Provide the ×ˢ notation (as used in the textbook) for our `cartesian` product.
notation:65 A:66 " ×ˢ " B:65 => SetTheory.Set.cartesian A B

/-- Target theorem (Exercise 3.5.4). -/
 theorem SetTheory.Set.inter_prod (A B C:Set) : (A ∩ B) ×ˢ C = (A ×ˢ C) 
    ∩ (B ×ˢ C) := by sorry

end Chapter3

open Classical in
/-- Exercise 8.3.2.  Some subtle type changes due to how sets are implemented in Mathlib. Also we shift the sequence `D` by one so that we can work in `Set A` rather than `Set B`. -/
theorem Schroder_Bernstein_lemma {X: Type} {A B C: Set X} (hAB: A ⊆ B) (hBC: B ⊆ C) (f: C ↪ A) :
  let D : ℕ → Set A := Nat.rec ((f.image ∘ ((B.embeddingOfSubset _ hBC).image)) {x:B | ↑x ∉ A}) (fun _ ↦ (f.image ∘ ((B.embeddingOfSubset _ hBC).image) ∘ (A.embeddingOfSubset _ hAB).image))
  Set.univ.PairwiseDisjoint D ∧
  let g : A → B := fun x ↦ if h: x ∈ ⋃ n, D n ∧ ∃ y:B, f ⟨↑y, hBC y.property⟩ = x then h.2.choose else ⟨ ↑x, hAB x.property ⟩
  Function.Bijective g
  := by
  sorry

end Chapter8

inductive BoundedInterval where
  | Ioo (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Icc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ioc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ico (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval

open BoundedInterval

@[coe]
def BoundedInterval.toSet (I: BoundedInterval) : Set ℝ := match I with
  | Ioo a b => .Ioo a b
  | Icc a b => .Icc a b
  | Ioc a b => .Ioc a b
  | Ico a b => .Ico a b

instance BoundedInterval.inst_coeSet : Coe BoundedInterval (Set ℝ) where
  coe := toSet

instance BoundedInterval.instMembership : Membership ℝ BoundedInterval where
  mem I x := x ∈ (I:Set ℝ)

instance BoundedInterval.instSubset : HasSubset BoundedInterval where
  Subset I J := ∀ x, x ∈ I → x ∈ J

@[ext]
structure Partition (I: BoundedInterval) where
  intervals : Finset BoundedInterval
  exists_unique (x:ℝ) (hx : x ∈ I) : ∃! J, J ∈ intervals ∧ x ∈ J
  contains (J : BoundedInterval) (hJ : J ∈ intervals) : J ⊆ I

instance Partition.instMembership (I: BoundedInterval) : Membership BoundedInterval (Partition I) where
  mem P J := J ∈ P.intervals

/-- Definition 11.2.1 -/
abbrev Constant {X Y:Type} (f: X → Y) : Prop := ∃ c, ∀ x, f x = c

abbrev ConstantOn (f: ℝ → ℝ) (X: Set ℝ) : Prop := Constant (fun x : X ↦ f ↑x)

/-- Definition 11.2.3 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantWith (f:ℝ → ℝ) {I: BoundedInterval} (P: Partition I) : Prop := ∀ J ∈ P, ConstantOn f (J:Set ℝ)

/-- Definition 11.2.5 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantOn (f:ℝ → ℝ) (I: BoundedInterval) : Prop := ∃ P : Partition I, PiecewiseConstantWith f P

/-- Lemma 11.2.8 / Exercise 11.2.2 -/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.smul {f: ℝ → ℝ} {I: BoundedInterval}
  (c:ℝ) (hf: PiecewiseConstantOn f I) : PiecewiseConstantOn (c • f) I := by
  sorry

end Chapter11

open Lean Elab Tactic in
macro "grind" : tactic => `(tactic| (repeat intro; simp [*]))

namespace Chapter7

open BigOperators

@[ext]
structure Series where
  m : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℝ
  vanish : ∀ n < m, seq n = 0

/-- Functions from ℕ to ℝ can be thought of as series. -/
instance Series.instCoe : Coe (ℕ → ℝ) Series where
  coe := fun a ↦ {
    m := 0
    seq n := if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
    vanish := by grind
  }

@[simp]
theorem Series.eval_coe (a: ℕ → ℝ) (n: ℕ) : (a: Series).seq n = a n := by simp

abbrev Series.mk' {m:ℤ} (a: { n // n ≥ m } → ℝ) : Series where
  m := m
  seq n := if h : n ≥ m then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by grind

/-- Definition 7.2.2 (Convergence of series) -/
abbrev Series.partial (s : Series) (N:ℤ) : ℝ := ∑ n ∈ Finset.Icc s.m N, s.seq n

abbrev Series.convergesTo (s : Series) (L:ℝ) : Prop := Filter.atTop.Tendsto (s.partial) (nhds L)

abbrev Series.converges (s : Series) : Prop := ∃ L, s.convergesTo L

/-- Definition 7.2.8 (Absolute convergence) -/
abbrev Series.abs (s:Series) : Series := mk' (m:=s.m) (fun n ↦ |s.seq n|)

abbrev Series.absConverges (s:Series) : Prop := s.abs.converges

end Chapter7

open Chapter7

  (ha': ¬ (a:Series).absConverges)  :
  ∃ f : ℕ → ℕ,  Bijective f ∧ atTop.Tendsto (fun N ↦ ((a ∘ f:Series).partial N : EReal)) (nhds ⊤) := by

import Mathlib.Tactic

/--
  This definition needs to be made outside of the Section 4.3 namespace for technical reasons.
-/
def Rat.Close (ε : ℚ) (x y:ℚ) := |x-y| ≤ ε

namespace Chapter5

/--
  Definition 5.1.1 (Sequence). To avoid some technicalities involving dependent types, we extend
  sequences by zero to the left of the starting point `n₀`.
-/
@[ext]
structure Sequence where
  n₀ : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℚ
  vanish : ∀ n < n₀, seq n = 0

/-- Sequences can be thought of as functions from ℤ to ℚ. -/
instance Sequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun Sequence (fun _ ↦ ℤ → ℚ) where
  coe := fun a ↦ a.seq

/--
Functions from ℕ to ℚ can be thought of as sequences starting from 0; `ofNatFun` performs this conversion.

The `coe` attribute allows the delaborator to print `Sequence.ofNatFun f` as `↑f`, which is more concise; you may safely remove this if you prefer the more explicit notation.
-/
@[coe]
def Sequence.ofNatFun (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Sequence where
    n₀ := 0
    seq n := if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
    vanish := by grind

/-- If `a : ℕ → ℚ` is used in a context where a `Sequence` is expected, automatically coerce `a` to `Sequence.ofNatFun a` (which will be pretty-printed as `↑a`) -/
instance : Coe (ℕ → ℚ) Sequence where
  coe := Sequence.ofNatFun

abbrev Sequence.mk' (n₀:ℤ) (a: { n // n ≥ n₀ } → ℚ) : Sequence where
  n₀ := n₀
  seq n := if h : n ≥ n₀ then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by grind

lemma Sequence.eval_mk {n n₀:ℤ} (a: { n // n ≥ n₀ } → ℚ) (h: n ≥ n₀) :
    (Sequence.mk' n₀ a) n = a ⟨ n, h ⟩ := by grind

@[simp]
lemma Sequence.eval_coe (n:ℕ) (a: ℕ → ℚ) : (a:Sequence) n = a n := by norm_cast

@[simp]
lemma Sequence.eval_coe_at_int (n:ℤ) (a: ℕ → ℚ) : (a:Sequence) n = if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0 := by norm_cast

@[simp]
lemma Sequence.n0_coe (a: ℕ → ℚ) : (a:Sequence).n₀ = 0 := by norm_cast

/--
  a.from n₁ starts `a:Sequence` from `n₁`.  It is intended for use when `n₁ ≥ n₀`, but returns
  the "junk" value of the original sequence `a` otherwise.
-/
abbrev Sequence.from (a:Sequence) (n₁:ℤ) : Sequence :=
  mk' (max a.n₀ n₁) (fun n ↦ a (n:ℤ))

lemma Sequence.from_eval (a:Sequence) {n₁ n:ℤ} (hn: n ≥ n₁) :
  (a.from n₁) n = a n := by simp [hn]; intro h; exact (a.vanish _ h).symm

end Chapter5

/-- A slight generalization of Definition 5.1.3 - definition of ε-steadiness for a sequence with an
arbitrary starting point n₀ -/
abbrev Rat.Steady (ε: ℚ) (a: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n ≥ a.n₀, ∀ m ≥ a.n₀, ε.Close (a n) (a m)

lemma Rat.steady_def (ε: ℚ) (a: Chapter5.Sequence) :
  ε.Steady a ↔ ∀ n ≥ a.n₀, ∀ m ≥ a.n₀, ε.Close (a n) (a m) := by rfl

/-- Definition 5.1.6 (Eventually ε-steady) -/
abbrev Rat.EventuallySteady (ε: ℚ) (a: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop := ∃ N ≥ a.n₀, ε.Steady (a.from N)

lemma Rat.eventuallySteady_def (ε: ℚ) (a: Chapter5.Sequence) :
  ε.EventuallySteady a ↔ ∃ N ≥ a.n₀, ε.Steady (a.from N) := by rfl

abbrev Rat.CloseSeq (ε: ℚ) (a b: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n, n ≥ a.n₀ → n ≥ b.n₀ → ε.Close (a n) (b n)

abbrev Rat.EventuallyClose (ε: ℚ) (a b: Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N, ε.CloseSeq (a.from N) (b.from N)

namespace Chapter5

abbrev Sequence.IsCauchy (a:Sequence) : Prop := ∀ ε > (0:ℚ), ε.EventuallySteady a

lemma Sequence.isCauchy_def (a:Sequence) :
  a.IsCauchy ↔ ∀ ε > (0:ℚ), ε.EventuallySteady a := by rfl

/-- A class of Cauchy sequences that start at zero -/
@[ext]
class CauchySequence extends Sequence where
  zero : n₀ = 0
  cauchy : toSequence.IsCauchy

/-- A sequence starting at zero that is Cauchy, can be viewed as a Cauchy sequence.-/
abbrev CauchySequence.mk' {a:ℕ → ℚ} (ha: (a:Sequence).IsCauchy) : CauchySequence where
  n₀ := 0
  seq := (a:Sequence).seq
  vanish := by aesop
  zero := rfl
  cauchy := ha

@[simp]
theorem CauchySequence.coe_eq {a:ℕ → ℚ} (ha: (a:Sequence).IsCauchy) :
    (mk' ha).toSequence = (a:Sequence) := rfl

instance CauchySequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun CauchySequence (fun _ ↦ ℕ → ℚ) where
  coe a n := a.toSequence (n:ℤ)

@[simp]
theorem CauchySequence.coe_to_sequence (a: CauchySequence) :
    ((a:ℕ → ℚ):Sequence) = a.toSequence := by
  apply Sequence.ext (by simp [Sequence.n0_coe, a.zero])
  ext n; by_cases h:n ≥ 0 <;> simp_all
  rw [a.vanish]; rwa [a.zero]

@[simp]
theorem CauchySequence.coe_coe {a:ℕ → ℚ} (ha: (a:Sequence).IsCauchy) : mk' ha = a := by rfl

/-- Definition 5.2.6 (Equivalent sequences) -/
abbrev Sequence.Equiv (a b: ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∀ ε > (0:ℚ), ε.EventuallyClose (a:Sequence) (b:Sequence)

/-- Proposition 5.3.3 / Exercise 5.3.1 -/
theorem Sequence.equiv_trans {a b c:ℕ → ℚ} (hab: Equiv a b) (hbc: Equiv b c) :
  Equiv a c := by sorry

/-- Proposition 5.3.3 / Exercise 5.3.1 -/
instance CauchySequence.instSetoid : Setoid CauchySequence where
  r := fun a b ↦ Sequence.Equiv a b
  iseqv := {
     refl := sorry
     symm := sorry
     trans := sorry
  }

/-- Every constant sequence is Cauchy -/
theorem Sequence.IsCauchy.const (a:ℚ) : ((fun _:ℕ ↦ a):Sequence).IsCauchy := by sorry

instance CauchySequence.instZero : Zero CauchySequence where
  zero := CauchySequence.mk' (a := fun _: ℕ ↦ 0) (Sequence.IsCauchy.const (0:ℚ))

abbrev Real := Quotient CauchySequence.instSetoid

open Classical in
/--
  It is convenient in Lean to assign the "dummy" value of 0 to `LIM a` when `a` is not Cauchy.
  This requires Classical logic, because the property of being Cauchy is not computable or
  decidable.
-/
noncomputable abbrev LIM (a:ℕ → ℚ) : Real :=
  Quotient.mk _ (if h : (a:Sequence).IsCauchy then CauchySequence.mk' h else (0:CauchySequence))

instance Real.instRatCast : RatCast Real where
  ratCast := fun q ↦
    Quotient.mk _ (CauchySequence.mk' (a := fun _ ↦ q) (Sequence.IsCauchy.const q))

instance Real.instOfNat {n:ℕ} : OfNat Real n where
  ofNat := ((n:ℚ):Real)

/-- Definition 5.4.1 (sequences bounded away from zero with sign). -/
abbrev BoundedAwayNeg (a:ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∃ (c:ℚ), c > 0 ∧ ∀ n, a n ≤ -c

abbrev Real.IsNeg (x:Real) : Prop :=
  ∃ a:ℕ → ℚ, BoundedAwayNeg a ∧ (a:Sequence).IsCauchy ∧ x = LIM a

/-- Proposition 5.4.4 (basic properties of positive reals) / Exercise 5.4.1 -/
 theorem Real.not_zero_neg (x:Real) : ¬(x = 0 ∧ x.IsNeg) := by sorry

end Chapter5

import Mathlib.Tactic
import Mathlib.Data.Real.Basic

/-!
Self-contained slice for Chapter 11, Sections 11.1–11.2, sufficient to typecheck
PiecewiseConstantOn.mul. We avoid importing any Analysis.Section_* modules.
-/

namespace Chapter11

inductive BoundedInterval where
  | Ioo (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Icc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ioc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ico (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval

open BoundedInterval

/-- There is a technical issue in that this coercion is not injective: the empty set is represented by multiple bounded intervals.  This causes some of the statements in this section to be a little uglier than necessary.-/
@[coe]
def BoundedInterval.toSet (I: BoundedInterval) : Set ℝ := match I with
  | Ioo a b => .Ioo a b
  | Icc a b => .Icc a b
  | Ioc a b => .Ioc a b
  | Ico a b => .Ico a b

instance BoundedInterval.inst_coeSet : Coe BoundedInterval (Set ℝ) where
  coe := toSet

instance BoundedInterval.instEmpty : EmptyCollection BoundedInterval where
  emptyCollection := Ioo 0 0

@[simp]
theorem BoundedInterval.coe_empty : ((∅ : BoundedInterval):Set ℝ) = ∅ := by
  simp [toSet]

instance BoundedInterval.instMembership : Membership ℝ BoundedInterval where
  mem I x := x ∈ (I:Set ℝ)

instance BoundedInterval.instSubset : HasSubset BoundedInterval where
  Subset I J := ∀ x, x ∈ I → x ∈ J

open Classical in
/-- This is to make Finsets of BoundedIntervals work properly -/
noncomputable instance BoundedInterval.decidableEq : DecidableEq BoundedInterval := instDecidableEqOfLawfulBEq

@[ext]
structure Partition (I: BoundedInterval) where
  intervals : Finset BoundedInterval
  exists_unique (x:ℝ) (hx : x ∈ I) : ∃! J, J ∈ intervals ∧ x ∈ J
  contains (J : BoundedInterval) (hJ : J ∈ intervals) : J ⊆ I

instance Partition.instMembership (I: BoundedInterval) : Membership BoundedInterval (Partition I) where
  mem P J := J ∈ P.intervals

/-- Definition 11.2.1 -/
abbrev Constant {X Y:Type} (f: X → Y) : Prop := ∃ c, ∀ x, f x = c

abbrev ConstantOn (f: ℝ → ℝ) (X: Set ℝ) : Prop := Constant (fun x : X ↦ f ↑x)

/-- Definition 11.2.3 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantWith (f:ℝ → ℝ) {I: BoundedInterval} (P: Partition I) : Prop := ∀ J ∈ P, ConstantOn f (J:Set ℝ)

/-- Definition 11.2.5 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantOn (f:ℝ → ℝ) (I: BoundedInterval) : Prop := ∃ P : Partition I, PiecewiseConstantWith f P

/-- Lemma 11.2.8 / Exercise 11.2.2 -/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.mul {f g: ℝ → ℝ} {I: BoundedInterval}
  (hf: PiecewiseConstantOn f I) (hg: PiecewiseConstantOn g I) : PiecewiseConstantOn (f * g) I := by
  sorry

def Rat.Close (ε : ℚ) (x y:ℚ) := |x-y| ≤ ε

theorem close_trans {ε δ x y z:ℚ} (hxy: ε.Close x y) (hyz: δ.Close y z) :
    (ε + δ).Close x z := by sorry

namespace Chapter8

/-- The definition of equal cardinality. For simplicity we restrict attention to the Type 0 universe.
This is analogous to `Chapter3.SetTheory.Set.EqualCard`, but we are not using the latter since
the Chapter 3 set theory is deprecated. -/
abbrev EqualCard (X Y : Type) : Prop := ∃ f : X → Y, Function.Bijective f

abbrev LeCard (X Y: Type) : Prop := ∃ f: X → Y, Function.Injective f

abbrev LtCard (X Y: Type) : Prop := LeCard X Y ∧ ¬ EqualCard X Y

end Chapter8

theorem Exercise_8_3_4a {X:Type} : LtCard X (Set X) := by sorry

theorem exists_function {X Y : Type} {P : X → Y → Prop} (h: ∀ x, ∃ y, P x y) :

import Mathlib.Tactic

/-- Definition from Section 4.3: eps-closeness on rationals -/
def Rat.Close (ε : ℚ) (x y : ℚ) := |x - y| ≤ ε

namespace Chapter5

@[ext]
structure Sequence where
  n₀ : ℤ
  seq : ℤ → ℚ
  vanish : ∀ n < n₀, seq n = 0

instance Sequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun Sequence (fun _ ↦ ℤ → ℚ) where
  coe := fun a ↦ a.seq

/-- Functions from ℕ to ℚ coerced to sequences starting at 0. -/
@[coe]
def Sequence.ofNatFun (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Sequence where
  n₀ := 0
  seq n := if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0
  vanish := by
    grind

instance : Coe (ℕ → ℚ) Sequence where
  coe := Sequence.ofNatFun

abbrev Sequence.mk' (n₀ : ℤ) (a : { n // n ≥ n₀ } → ℚ) : Sequence where
  n₀ := n₀
  seq n := if h : n ≥ n₀ then a ⟨n, h⟩ else 0
  vanish := by
    grind

@[simp]
lemma Sequence.eval_coe_at_int (n : ℤ) (a : ℕ → ℚ) :
    (a : Sequence) n = if n ≥ 0 then a n.toNat else 0 := by
  norm_cast

@[simp]
lemma Sequence.n0_coe (a : ℕ → ℚ) : (a : Sequence).n₀ = 0 := by
  norm_cast

/-- Start a sequence from a later index. -/
abbrev Sequence.from (a : Sequence) (n₁ : ℤ) : Sequence :=
  mk' (max a.n₀ n₁) (fun n ↦ a (n : ℤ))

end Chapter5

/-- ε-steadiness of a sequence (Section 5.1). -/
abbrev Rat.Steady (ε : ℚ) (a : Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n ≥ a.n₀, ∀ m ≥ a.n₀, ε.Close (a n) (a m)

/-- Eventually ε-steadiness (reusing `Sequence.from`). -/
abbrev Rat.EventuallySteady (ε : ℚ) (a : Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N ≥ a.n₀, ε.Steady (a.from N)

namespace Chapter5

/-- Eventually ε-steadiness (Section 5.1). -/
abbrev Sequence.IsCauchy (a : Sequence) : Prop := ∀ ε > (0 : ℚ), ε.EventuallySteady a

end Chapter5

/-- ε-close sequences (Section 5.2). -/
abbrev Rat.CloseSeq (ε : ℚ) (a b : Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∀ n, n ≥ a.n₀ → n ≥ b.n₀ → ε.Close (a n) (b n)

/-- Eventually ε-close sequences (Section 5.2). -/
abbrev Rat.EventuallyClose (ε : ℚ) (a b : Chapter5.Sequence) : Prop :=
  ∃ N, ε.CloseSeq (a.from N) (b.from N)

namespace Chapter5

/-- Equivalent sequences (Section 5.2). -/
abbrev Sequence.Equiv (a b : ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∀ ε > (0 : ℚ), ε.EventuallyClose (a : Sequence) (b : Sequence)

/-- Cauchy sequences starting at 0 (Section 5.3). -/
@[ext]
class CauchySequence extends Sequence where
  zero : n₀ = 0
  cauchy : toSequence.IsCauchy

instance CauchySequence.instCoeFun : CoeFun CauchySequence (fun _ ↦ ℕ → ℚ) where
  coe a n := a.toSequence (n : ℤ)

/-- View a Cauchy sequence coming from a Cauchy ℕ-indexed sequence. -/
abbrev CauchySequence.mk' {a : ℕ → ℚ} (ha : (a : Sequence).IsCauchy) : CauchySequence where
  n₀ := 0
  seq := (a : Sequence).seq
  vanish := by aesop
  zero := rfl
  cauchy := ha

/-- Setoid of Cauchy sequences modulo eventual closeness (Section 5.3). -/
instance CauchySequence.instSetoid : Setoid CauchySequence where
  r := fun a b ↦ Sequence.Equiv a b
  iseqv :=
    { refl := sorry
      symm := sorry
      trans := sorry }

/-- Constant sequences are Cauchy. -/
lemma Sequence.IsCauchy.const (a : ℚ) : ((fun _ : ℕ ↦ a) : Sequence).IsCauchy := by
  sorry

/-- Zero Cauchy sequence. -/
instance CauchySequence.instZero : Zero CauchySequence where
  zero := CauchySequence.mk' (a := fun _ : ℕ ↦ 0) (Sequence.IsCauchy.const (0 : ℚ))

/-- The real numbers as equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences (Section 5.3). -/
abbrev Real := Quotient CauchySequence.instSetoid

open Classical in
/-- Formal limit of a sequence, defaulting to 0 if not Cauchy (Section 5.3). -/
noncomputable abbrev LIM (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Real :=
  Quotient.mk _ (if h : (a : Sequence).IsCauchy then CauchySequence.mk' h else (0 : CauchySequence))

/-- Sequences bounded away from zero on the positive side (Section 5.4). -/
abbrev BoundedAwayPos (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∃ c : ℚ, c > 0 ∧ ∀ n, a n ≥ c

/-- Sequences bounded away from zero on the negative side (Section 5.4). -/
abbrev BoundedAwayNeg (a : ℕ → ℚ) : Prop :=
  ∃ c : ℚ, c > 0 ∧ ∀ n, a n ≤ -c

namespace Real

/-- Positive real (via a positive, Cauchy rational sequence with given limit). -/
abbrev IsPos (x : Chapter5.Real) : Prop :=
  ∃ a : ℕ → ℚ, BoundedAwayPos a ∧ (a : Sequence).IsCauchy ∧ x = LIM a

/-- Negative real (via a negative, Cauchy rational sequence with given limit). -/
abbrev IsNeg (x : Chapter5.Real) : Prop :=
  ∃ a : ℕ → ℚ, BoundedAwayNeg a ∧ (a : Sequence).IsCauchy ∧ x = LIM a

end Real

/-- Proposition 5.4.4 (basic properties of positive reals) / Exercise 5.4.1 -/
 theorem Real.not_pos_neg (x:Real) : ¬(x.IsPos ∧ x.IsNeg) := by
  sorry

end Chapter5

import Mathlib.Tactic

/-!
Self-contained extraction for Chapter 3 Section 3.5 target theorem.
-/

namespace Chapter3

universe u v

/-- The axioms of Zermelo-Frankel theory with atoms.  -/
class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u -- Axiom 3.1
  Object : Type v -- Axiom 3.1
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.1
  mem : Object → Set → Prop -- Axiom 3.1
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y -- Axiom 3.2
  emptyset: Set -- Axiom 3.3
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset -- Axiom 3.3
  singleton : Object → Set -- Axiom 3.4
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y -- Axiom 3.4
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.5
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y) -- Axiom 3.5
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set -- Axiom 3.6
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x -- Axiom 3.6
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set -- Axiom 3.7
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y -- Axiom 3.7
  nat : Set -- Axiom 3.8
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat) -- Axiom 3.8
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A 
      ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S -- Axiom 3.9
  pow : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.11
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.11
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F -- Axiom 3.11
  union : Set → Set -- Axiom 3.12
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A -- Axiom 3.12

export SetTheory (Set Object)

variable [SetTheory]

/-- Definition 3.1.1 (objects can be elements of sets) -/
instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := SetTheory.mem x X

/-- Axiom 3.1 (Sets are objects)-/
instance SetTheory.sets_are_objects : Coe Set Object where
  coe X := SetTheory.set_to_object X

/-- Axiom 3.1 (Sets are objects)-/
@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.coe_eq_iff (X Y:Set) : (X: Object) = (Y: Object) ↔  X = Y := by
  constructor
  · intro h; exact SetTheory.set_to_object.inj' h
  · rintro rfl; rfl

/-- Axiom 3.2 (Equality of sets).  The `[ext]` tag allows the `ext` tactic to work for sets. -/
@[ext]
theorem SetTheory.Set.ext {X Y:Set} (h: ∀ x, x ∈ X ↔ x ∈ Y) : X = Y :=
  SetTheory.extensionality _ _ h

instance SetTheory.Set.instEmpty : EmptyCollection Set where
  emptyCollection := SetTheory.emptyset

open SetTheory.Set

@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.not_mem_empty : ∀ x, x ∉ (∅:Set) := SetTheory.emptyset_mem

instance SetTheory.Set.instSingleton : Singleton Object Set where
  singleton := SetTheory.singleton

instance SetTheory.Set.instUnion : Union Set where
  union := SetTheory.union_pair

@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.mem_union (x:Object) (X Y:Set) : x ∈ (X ∪ Y) ↔ (x ∈ X ∨ x ∈ Y) :=
  SetTheory.union_pair_axiom X Y x

instance SetTheory.Set.instInsert : Insert Object Set where
  insert x X := {x} ∪ X

/-- This defines the subtype `A.toSubtype` for any `A:Set`. -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.toSubtype (A:Set) := Subtype (fun x ↦ x ∈ A)

instance : CoeSort (Set) (Type v) where
  coe A := A.toSubtype

/-- Elements of a set (implicitly coerced to a subtype) are also elements of the set. -/
lemma SetTheory.Set.subtype_property (A:Set) (x:A) : x.val ∈ A := x.property

/-- Axiom 3.6 (axiom of specification) -/
@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.specification_axiom'' {A:Set} (P: A → Prop) (x:Object) :
    x ∈ SetTheory.specify A P ↔ ∃ h:x ∈ A, P ⟨ x, h ⟩ := by
  constructor
  · intro h
    have h' := (SetTheory.specification_axiom A P).1 x h
    refine ⟨h', ?_⟩
    have := (SetTheory.specification_axiom A P).2 ⟨x, h'⟩
    simpa using this.mp (by
      -- show mem x.val (SetTheory.specify A P) from h; but x.val = x
      simpa using h)
  · rintro ⟨h, hP⟩
    have := (SetTheory.specification_axiom A P).2 ⟨x, h⟩
    simpa using this.mpr hP

/-- Replacement packaged for sets. -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.replace (A:Set) {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP : ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set := SetTheory.replace A P hP

/-- Axiom 3.7 (Axiom of replacement) -/
@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.replacement_axiom {A:Set} {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') (y:Object) :
    y ∈ A.replace hP ↔ ∃ x, P x y := SetTheory.replacement_axiom A P hP y

/-- Axiom 3.12 (Union) -/
@[simp]
lemma SetTheory.Set.union_axiom (A: Set) (x:Object) :
    x ∈ SetTheory.union A ↔ ∃ (S:Set), x ∈ S 
      ∧ (S:Object) ∈ A :=
  SetTheory.union_axiom A x

/-- Definition 3.5.1 (Ordered pair). -/
@[ext]
structure OrderedPair where
  fst: Object
  snd: Object

/-- Definition 3.5.1 (Ordered pair) -/
@[simp]
theorem OrderedPair.eq (x y x' y' : Object) :
    (⟨ x, y ⟩ : OrderedPair) = (⟨ x', y' ⟩ : OrderedPair) ↔ x = x' ∧ y = y' := by
  aesop

/-- Exercise 3.5.1, first part -/
def OrderedPair.toObject : OrderedPair ↪ Object where
  toFun p := ({ (({p.fst}:Set):Object), (({p.fst, p.snd}:Set):Object) }:Set)
  inj' := by sorry

instance OrderedPair.inst_coeObject : Coe OrderedPair Object where
  coe := OrderedPair.toObject

/--
  A technical operation, turning a object `x` and a set `Y` to a set `{x} × Y`, needed to define
  the full Cartesian product
-/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.slice (x:Object) (Y:Set) : Set :=
  Y.replace (P := fun y z ↦ z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair)) (by
    intro a b b' h; exact h.1.trans h.2.symm)

@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.mem_slice (x z:Object) (Y:Set) :
    z ∈ (SetTheory.Set.slice x Y) ↔ ∃ y:Y, z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair) :=
  SetTheory.Set.replacement_axiom (A:=Y) (P:= fun y z ↦ z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair)) _ z

/-- Definition 3.5.4 (Cartesian product) -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.cartesian (X Y:Set) : Set :=
  SetTheory.union (X.replace (P := fun x z ↦ z = slice x Y) (by
    intro a b b' h; exact h.1.trans h.2.symm))

/-- This instance enables the ×ˢ notation for Cartesian product. -/
instance SetTheory.Set.inst_SProd : SProd Set Set Set where
  sprod := SetTheory.Set.cartesian

@[simp]
theorem SetTheory.Set.mem_cartesian (z:Object) (X Y:Set) :
    z ∈ X ×ˢ Y ↔ ∃ x:X, ∃ y:Y, z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair) := by
  simp only [SProd.sprod, SetTheory.Set.union_axiom]; constructor
  · intro hx
    rcases hx with ⟨S, hzS, hS⟩
    -- unpack hS via replacement on X
    have hxrep := (SetTheory.Set.replacement_axiom
      (A:=X) (P:= fun x z ↦ z = slice x Y)
      (by intro a b b' h; exact h.1.trans h.2.symm) (y:=(S: _))).mp hS
    rcases hxrep with ⟨x, hx⟩
    -- hx : S = slice x Y
    have hSeq : S = slice x Y := by
      -- hx is equality in Object; convert to Set equality
      exact (SetTheory.set_to_object.inj' hx)
    subst hSeq
    -- now hzS : z ∈ slice x Y
    have hz' := (SetTheory.Set.replacement_axiom
      (A:=Y) (P:= fun y z ↦ z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair))
      (by intro a b b' h; exact h.1.trans h.2.symm) (y:=z)).mp hzS
    rcases hz' with ⟨y, hy⟩
    rcases hy with rfl
    exact ⟨x, ⟨y, rfl⟩⟩
  · rintro ⟨x, y, rfl⟩
    -- show (⟨x, y⟩ : OrderedPair) ∈ union (X.replace ...)
    -- take S := slice x Y
    refine ⟨slice x Y, ?_, ?_⟩
    · -- membership in slice
      exact (SetTheory.Set.replacement_axiom
        (A:=Y) (P:= fun y z ↦ z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair))
        (by intro a b b' h; exact h.1.trans h.2.symm) (y:=(⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair))).mpr
        ⟨y, rfl⟩
    · -- show (slice x Y : Object) ∈ X.replace ...
      have := (SetTheory.Set.replacement_axiom
        (A:=X) (P:= fun x z ↦ z = slice x Y)
        (by intro a b b' h; exact h.1.trans h.2.symm) (y:=(slice x Y : _))).mpr
      exact this ⟨x, rfl⟩

/-- Projections from a Cartesian product. -/
noncomputable abbrev SetTheory.Set.fst {X Y:Set} (z:X ×ˢ Y) : X :=
  ((SetTheory.Set.mem_cartesian _ _ _).mp z.property).choose

noncomputable abbrev SetTheory.Set.snd {X Y:Set} (z:X ×ˢ Y) : Y :=
  (exists_comm.mp ((SetTheory.Set.mem_cartesian _ _ _).mp z.property)).choose

/-- Exercise 3.5.7 -/
 theorem SetTheory.Set.direct_sum {X Y Z:Set} (f: Z → X) (g: Z → Y) :
    ∃! h: Z → X ×ˢ Y, fst ∘ h = f ∧ snd ∘ h = g := by
  sorry

end Chapter3

import Mathlib.Tactic

/-!
Self-contained slice for Chapter 3 Section 3.5 target theorem
-/

namespace Chapter3

universe u v

/-- The axioms of Zermelo-Frankel theory with atoms (exactly as in Section_3_1). -/
class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u -- Axiom 3.1
  Object : Type v -- Axiom 3.1
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.1
  mem : Object → Set → Prop -- Axiom 3.1
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y -- Axiom 3.2
  emptyset: Set -- Axiom 3.3
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset -- Axiom 3.3
  singleton : Object → Set -- Axiom 3.4
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y -- Axiom 3.4
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.5
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y) -- Axiom 3.5
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set -- Axiom 3.6
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x -- Axiom 3.6
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set -- Axiom 3.7
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y -- Axiom 3.7
  nat : Set -- Axiom 3.8
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat) -- Axiom 3.8
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S -- Axiom 3.9
  pow : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.11
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.11
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F -- Axiom 3.11
  union : Set → Set -- Axiom 3.12
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A -- Axiom 3.12

export SetTheory (Set Object nat)

variable [SetTheory]

/-- Axiom 3.1 (objects can be elements of sets) -/
instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := mem x X

/-- Axiom 3.1 (Sets are objects)-/ 
instance SetTheory.sets_are_objects : Coe Set Object where
  coe X := SetTheory.set_to_object X

/-- Axiom 3.2 (Equality of sets). -/
@[ext]
 theorem SetTheory.Set.ext {X Y:Set} (h: ∀ x, x ∈ X ↔ x ∈ Y) : X = Y :=
  SetTheory.extensionality _ _ h

instance SetTheory.Set.instEmpty : EmptyCollection Set where
  emptyCollection := SetTheory.emptyset

/-- Elements of a set can be bundled as a subtype. -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.toSubtype (A:Set) := Subtype (fun x ↦ x ∈ A)

instance : CoeSort (Set) (Type v) where
  coe A := SetTheory.Set.toSubtype A

abbrev SetTheory.Set.specify (A:Set) (P: A → Prop) : Set := SetTheory.specify A P

/-- Axiom 3.12 (Union) -/
 theorem SetTheory.Set.union_axiom (A: Set) (x:Object) :
    x ∈ SetTheory.union A ↔ ∃ (S:Set), x ∈ S ∧ (S:Object) ∈ A :=
  SetTheory.union_axiom A x

/-- Replacement packaged under Set namespace. -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.replace (A:Set) {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP : ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set := SetTheory.replace A P hP

/-- Axiom 3.7 (Axiom of replacement) -/
 theorem SetTheory.Set.replacement_axiom {A:Set} {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') (y:Object) :
    y ∈ A.replace hP ↔ ∃ x, P x y := SetTheory.replacement_axiom A P hP y

/-- The set of natural numbers (as a SetTheory.Set). -/
abbrev Nat := SetTheory.nat

/-- Axiom 3.8 (Axiom of infinity) -/
 def SetTheory.Set.nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Nat := SetTheory.nat_equiv

/-- Coercions between Set-theoretic naturals and ℕ. -/
instance SetTheory.Set.toNat : Coe Nat ℕ where
  coe n := (SetTheory.Set.nat_equiv).symm n

/-- Power set structure on Set. -/
instance SetTheory.Set.inst_pow : Pow Set Set where
  pow := SetTheory.pow

/-- Coercion from functions between sets to objects (from Section_3_4). -/
@[coe]
 def SetTheory.Set.coe_of_fun {X Y : Set} (f : X → Y) : Object :=
  SetTheory.function_to_object X Y f

/-- This coercion has to be a `CoeOut` rather than a `Coe` (as in Section_3_4). -/
instance SetTheory.Set.inst_coe_of_fun {X Y:Set} : CoeOut (X → Y) Object where
  coe := SetTheory.Set.coe_of_fun

/-- Indexed union (as in Section_3_4). -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.iUnion (I: Set) (A: I → Set) : Set :=
  SetTheory.union (SetTheory.Set.replace I (P := fun α S ↦ S = A α)
    (by
      intro _ _ _ h
      rcases h with ⟨h1, h2⟩
      exact h1.trans h2.symm))

/-- Membership in an indexed union (from Section_3_4). -/
 theorem SetTheory.Set.mem_iUnion {I:Set} (A: I → Set) (x:Object) :
    x ∈ iUnion I A ↔ ∃ α:I, x ∈ A α := by
  -- Original proof uses union_axiom and replacement_axiom; we elide here.
  sorry

open SetTheory.Set

/-- Definition 3.5.6. Tuple (from Section_3_5). -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.tuple {I:Set} {X: I → Set} (x: ∀ i, X i) : Object :=
  ((fun i ↦ ⟨ x i, by rw [mem_iUnion]; use i; exact (x i).property ⟩):I → iUnion I X)

/-- Definition 3.5.6. iProd (from Section_3_5). -/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.iProd {I: Set} (X: I → Set) : Set :=
  ((iUnion I X)^I).specify (fun t ↦ ∃ x : ∀ i, X i, t = tuple x)

/-- Finite set-theoretic analogue of Fin (from Section_3_5). -/

  sorry

namespace Chapter11

-- Section 11.1: Bounded intervals and partitions (minimal slice)

inductive BoundedInterval where
  | Ioo (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Icc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ioc (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval
  | Ico (a b:ℝ) : BoundedInterval

open BoundedInterval

/-- Coercion of a bounded interval to the corresponding subset of `ℝ`. -/
@[coe]
def BoundedInterval.toSet (I: BoundedInterval) : Set ℝ := match I with
  | Ioo a b => Set.Ioo a b
  | Icc a b => Set.Icc a b
  | Ioc a b => Set.Ioc a b
  | Ico a b => Set.Ico a b

instance BoundedInterval.inst_coeSet : Coe BoundedInterval (Set ℝ) where
  coe := toSet

/-- Membership of a real number in a bounded interval. -/
instance BoundedInterval.instMembership : Membership ℝ BoundedInterval where
  mem I x := x ∈ (I : Set ℝ)

/-- Subset relation on bounded intervals, via their underlying sets. -/
instance BoundedInterval.instSubset : HasSubset BoundedInterval where
  Subset I J := ∀ x, x ∈ I → x ∈ J

@[ext]
structure Partition (I: BoundedInterval) where
  intervals : Finset BoundedInterval
  exists_unique (x:ℝ) (hx : x ∈ I) : ∃! J, J ∈ intervals ∧ x ∈ J
  contains (J : BoundedInterval) (hJ : J ∈ intervals) : J ⊆ I

instance Partition.instMembership (I: BoundedInterval) : Membership BoundedInterval (Partition I) where
  mem P J := J ∈ P.intervals


-- Section 11.2: Piecewise constant functions (minimal slice)

open BoundedInterval

/-- Definition 11.2.1 -/
abbrev Constant {X Y:Type} (f: X → Y) : Prop := ∃ c, ∀ x, f x = c

abbrev ConstantOn (f: ℝ → ℝ) (X: Set ℝ) : Prop := Constant (fun x : X ↦ f ↑x)

/-- Definition 11.2.3 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantWith (f:ℝ → ℝ) {I: BoundedInterval} (P: Partition I) : Prop :=
  ∀ J ∈ P, ConstantOn f (J:Set ℝ)

/-- Definition 11.2.5 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/
abbrev PiecewiseConstantOn (f:ℝ → ℝ) (I: BoundedInterval) : Prop :=
  ∃ P : Partition I, PiecewiseConstantWith f P

/-- Lemma 11.2.8 / Exercise 11.2.2.  I believe the hypothesis that `g` does not vanish is not needed. -/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.div {f g: ℝ → ℝ} {I: BoundedInterval}
  (hf: PiecewiseConstantOn f I) (hg: PiecewiseConstantOn f I) : PiecewiseConstantOn (f / g) I := by
  sorry

end Chapter11

namespace Chapter3

universe u v

class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u
  Object : Type v
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object
  mem : Object → Set → Prop
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y
  emptyset: Set
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset
  singleton : Object → Set
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y)
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y
  nat : Set
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat)
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S
  pow : Set → Set → Set
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F
  union : Set → Set
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A

export SetTheory (Set Object)

variable [SetTheory]

instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := SetTheory.mem x X

instance SetTheory.sets_are_objects : Coe Set Object where
  coe X := SetTheory.set_to_object X

namespace SetTheory.Set

abbrev toSubtype (A:Set) := Subtype (fun x ↦ x ∈ A)

instance : CoeSort (Set) (Type v) where
  coe A := toSubtype A

abbrev specify (A:Set) (P: A → Prop) : Set := SetTheory.specify A P

instance instSingleton : Singleton Object Set where
  singleton := SetTheory.singleton

instance instUnion : Union Set where
  union := SetTheory.union_pair

instance instInsert : Insert Object Set where
  insert x X := {x} ∪ X

instance instSDiff : SDiff Set where
  sdiff X Y := X.specify (fun x ↦ x.val ∉ Y)

abbrev replace (A:Set) {P: A → Object → Prop}
  (hP : ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set := SetTheory.replace A P hP

end SetTheory.Set

open SetTheory.Set

@[ext]
structure OrderedPair where
  fst: Object
  snd: Object

/-- Exercise 3.5.1, first part -/
def OrderedPair.toObject : OrderedPair ↪ Object where
  toFun p := ({ (({p.fst}:Set):Object), (({p.fst, p.snd}:Set):Object) }:Set)
  inj' := by sorry

instance OrderedPair.inst_coeObject : Coe OrderedPair Object where
  coe := OrderedPair.toObject

abbrev SetTheory.Set.slice (x:Object) (Y:Set) : Set :=
  Y.replace (P := fun y z ↦ z = (⟨x, y⟩:OrderedPair)) (by grind)

abbrev SetTheory.Set.cartesian (X Y:Set) : Set :=
  SetTheory.union (X.replace (P := fun x z ↦ z = slice x Y) (by intro _ _ _ ⟨h1, h2⟩; exact h1.trans h2.symm))

instance SetTheory.Set.inst_SProd : SProd Set Set Set where
  sprod := SetTheory.Set.cartesian

def SetTheory.Set.diff_of_prod :
  Decidable (∀ (A B C D:Set), (A ×ˢ B) \ (C ×ˢ D) = (A \ C) ×ˢ (B \ D)) := by

/-- Definition 11.2.1 -/
abbrev Constant {X Y:Type} (f: X → Y) : Prop := ∃ c, ∀ x, f x = c

open Classical in
noncomputable abbrev constant_value {X Y:Type} [hY: Nonempty Y] (f:X → Y) : Y :=
  if h: Constant f then h.choose else hY.some

abbrev ConstantOn (f: ℝ → ℝ) (X: Set ℝ) : Prop := Constant (fun x : X ↦ f ↑x)

noncomputable abbrev constant_value_on (f:ℝ → ℝ) (X: Set ℝ) : ℝ := constant_value (fun x : X ↦ f ↑x)

/-- Definition 11.2.3 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/

  ∀ J ∈ P, ConstantOn f (J:Set ℝ)

/-- Definition 11.2.5 (Piecewise constant functions I) -/

  ∃ P : Partition I, PiecewiseConstantWith f P

/-- Definition 11.2.9 (Piecewise constant integral I)-/
noncomputable abbrev PiecewiseConstantWith.integ (f:ℝ → ℝ) {I: BoundedInterval} (P: Partition I)  :
  ℝ := ∑ J ∈ P.intervals, constant_value_on f (J:Set ℝ) * |J|ₗ

open Classical in
/-- Definition 11.2.14 (Piecewise constant integral II)  -/
noncomputable abbrev PiecewiseConstantOn.integ (f:ℝ → ℝ) (I: BoundedInterval) :
  ℝ := if h: PiecewiseConstantOn f I then PiecewiseConstantWith.integ f h.choose else 0

/-- Theorem 11.2.16 (a) (Laws of integration) / Exercise 11.2.4 -/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.integ_add {f g: ℝ → ℝ} {I: BoundedInterval}
  (hf: PiecewiseConstantOn f I) (hg: PiecewiseConstantOn g I) :
  integ (f + g) I = integ f I + integ g I := by
  sorry

namespace Chapter3

universe u v

/-- The axioms of Zermelo-Frankel theory with atoms.  -/
class SetTheory where
  Set : Type u -- Axiom 3.1
  Object : Type v -- Axiom 3.1
  set_to_object : Set ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.1
  mem : Object → Set → Prop -- Axiom 3.1
  extensionality X Y : (∀ x, mem x X ↔ mem x Y) → X = Y -- Axiom 3.2
  emptyset: Set -- Axiom 3.3
  emptyset_mem x : ¬ mem x emptyset -- Axiom 3.3
  singleton : Object → Set -- Axiom 3.4
  singleton_axiom x y : mem x (singleton y) ↔ x = y -- Axiom 3.4
  union_pair : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.5
  union_pair_axiom X Y x : mem x (union_pair X Y) ↔ (mem x X ∨ mem x Y) -- Axiom 3.5
  specify A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) : Set -- Axiom 3.6
  specification_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Prop) :
    (∀ x, mem x (specify A P) → mem x A) ∧ ∀ x, mem x.val (specify A P) ↔ P x -- Axiom 3.6
  replace A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : Set -- Axiom 3.7
  replacement_axiom A (P: Subtype (mem . A) → Object → Prop)
    (hP: ∀ x y y', P x y ∧ P x y' → y = y') : ∀ y, mem y (replace A P hP) ↔ ∃ x, P x y -- Axiom 3.7
  nat : Set -- Axiom 3.8
  nat_equiv : ℕ ≃ Subtype (mem . nat) -- Axiom 3.8
  regularity_axiom A (hA : ∃ x, mem x A) :
    ∃ x, mem x A ∧ ∀ S, x = set_to_object S → ¬ ∃ y, mem y A ∧ mem y S -- Axiom 3.9
  pow : Set → Set → Set -- Axiom 3.11
  function_to_object (X: Set) (Y: Set) :
    (Subtype (mem . X) → Subtype (mem . Y)) ↪ Object -- Axiom 3.11
  powerset_axiom (X: Set) (Y: Set) (F:Object) :
    mem F (pow X Y) ↔ ∃ f: Subtype (mem . Y) → Subtype (mem . X),
    function_to_object Y X f = F -- Axiom 3.11
  union : Set → Set -- Axiom 3.12
  union_axiom A x : mem x (union A) ↔ ∃ S, mem x S ∧ mem (set_to_object S) A -- Axiom 3.12

-- This enables one to use `Set` and `Object` instead of `SetTheory.Set` and `SetTheory.Object`.
export SetTheory (Set Object)

-- This instance implicitly imposes the axioms of Zermelo-Frankel set theory with atoms.
variable [SetTheory]

/-- Definition 3.1.1 (objects can be elements of sets) -/
instance SetTheory.objects_mem_sets : Membership Object Set where
  mem X x := SetTheory.mem x X

/--
  This defines the subtype `A.toSubtype` for any `A:Set`.
  Note that `A.toSubtype` gives you a type, similar to how `Object` or `Set` are types.
  A value `x'` of type `A.toSubtype` combines some `x: Object` with a proof that `hx: x ∈ A`.

  To produce an element `x'` of this subtype, use `⟨ x, hx ⟩`, where `x: Object` and `hx: x ∈ A`.
  The object `x` associated to a subtype element `x'` is recovered as `x'.val`, and
  the property `hx` that `x` belongs to `A` is recovered as `x'.property`.
-/
abbrev SetTheory.Set.toSubtype (A:Set) := Subtype (fun x ↦ x ∈ A)

instance : CoeSort (Set) (Type v) where
  coe A := A.toSubtype

@[coe]
def SetTheory.Set.coe_of_fun {X Y:Set} (f: X → Y) : Object :=
  SetTheory.function_to_object X Y f

/-- This coercion has to be a `CoeOut` rather than a
`Coe` because the input type `X → Y` contains
parameters not present in the output type `Output` -/
instance SetTheory.Set.inst_coe_of_fun {X Y:Set} : CoeOut (X → Y) Object where
  coe := SetTheory.Set.coe_of_fun

/--
  Exercise 3.5.11. This trivially follows from `SetTheory.Set.powerset_axiom`, but the
  exercise is to derive it from `SetTheory.Set.exists_powerset` instead.
-/
theorem SetTheory.Set.powerset_axiom' (X Y:Set) :
    ∃! S:Set, ∀(F:Object), F ∈ S ↔ ∃ f: Y → X, f = F := by
  sorry

end Chapter3
